schools.summary: Gill: spoken either immediately after the death of Nadab and Abihu, so the chapter stands next to ch. 10, or after the laws of uncleanness, that Aaron be cautioned that he die not as his sons died.

Keil & Delitzsch: the previous sacrifices did not complete the reconciliation between the holy God and a nation still involved in sin and uncleanness; many sins remained unacknowledged and unexpiated, so a yearly all-comprehending day of atonement was required.

themes: after the death of the two sons of Aaron
the daily sacrifices left sin unexpiated; a yearly atonement needed
reverence in approaching God

schools.summary: Gill: the charge to Aaron was binding on all high priests in succession from him; he might not come into the most holy place at all times, but only on the Day of Atonement.

Keil & Delitzsch: only 'with this' (bĕzo'th) — with the appointed sacrifices, garments, purifications and means of expiation — might Aaron enter the inmost division of the tabernacle, the holy of holies; God appeared in the cloud upon the mercy seat.

themes: not at all times into the holy place within the vail
that he die not
God appears in the cloud upon the mercy seat
the way into the holiest not yet made manifest (Heb. 9:7-8)

schools.summary: Gill: Aaron came into the most holy place after he had offered the daily morning sacrifice, burned the incense and trimmed the lamps.
Clarke: the bullock was a sin offering for himself, his family and the whole priesthood; the ram a burnt offering signifying that he and his associates were wholly consecrated to the ministry.

Ellicott: 'thus' = 'with this,' i.e. with the following sacrifices, ritual and vestments; in the morning service he wore the golden vestments, changed for the white garments before the special ritual.

themes: a young bullock for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering
sin first put away, then entire consecration
after the daily morning sacrifice

schools.summary: Gill: the holy linen coat was such as the high priest wore in common with the other priests.

Keil & Delitzsch: the linen coat, breeches, girdle and mitre were the holy garments; he was to wash his flesh in water before putting them on, wearing not the golden state-costume but the white garments for the expiatory ritual.

themes: the holy linen coat
wash his flesh in water
the white garments of humiliation, not the golden robes

schools.summary: Gill: on the Day of Atonement the high priest had to do only with the Israel of God, the elect given Christ by the Father, for whom Christ offered himself and made reconciliation.

Henry: for seven days before, the high priest retired to a chamber of the temple to prepare, doing the inferior priests' work and having the institution read to him again and again, that he might be fully apprised of the whole method.

themes: two kids of the goats for a sin offering, and one ram for a burnt offering
the high priest had to do with the Israel of God
seven days' preparation

schools.summary: Gill: 'for himself' means at his own expense and for his own family — the bullock wholly of his own money, not the people's; he made atonement for himself and his house.
Ellicott: his own sins had first to be expiated before he could offer the atoning sacrifices for the people (cf. Heb. 5:3; 9:7).

themes: make an atonement for himself, and for his house
the priest must atone for his own sin first
the contrast with the sinless High Priest (Heb. 7:27)

schools.summary: Gill: the two goats are a single emblem of Christ — the clean creature fit for food denoting his purity and his being wholesome food to faith; the number two figuring either his two natures (the live goat the impassible divine nature, the slain goat the suffering human) or his death and risen life.
Keil & Delitzsch: the two goats were presented before the LORD at the door of the tabernacle before the lots were cast.

themes: the two goats... present them before the LORD
two goats forming one sin offering
figuring death and the carrying-away of sin

schools.summary: Gill: gives the Mishnaic account — the high priest shook the urn (Calphi) and drew two box-tree lots, one inscribed 'for the LORD,' the other 'for Azazel,' setting them upon the two goats.
Clarke: reads la-azazel as 'for the scape-goat,' the dismissed or sent-away goat, distinguishing it from the goat offered in sacrifice.

sides.position: A common noun — 'the goat that departs,' the dismissed or sent-away goat
A place in the wilderness, a precipice; or an evil spirit/demon dwelling there

themes: one lot for the LORD, and the other lot for the scapegoat
the lots cast by God's appointment
the disputed name Azazel

schools.summary: Gill: the high priest lifted up the lot, laid it on the head of the goat, and brought it to the altar to be sacrificed as the Lord's goat.
Ellicott: after the lots fell the goats were distinguished by marks (a red thread on the one for Jehovah); 'offer' means appoint or present, the actual offering up taking place afterward (v.15).

themes: the goat upon which the LORD's lot fell
offered for a sin offering
the type of Christ offered for sin

schools.summary: Gill: the scapegoat (for Azazel) is presented alive and is a distinct figure from the slain goat.

Clarke: azazel from 'az (goat) and azal (to dismiss) — the dismissed or sent-away goat; most ancient nations had vicarious rites transferring a community's guilt to a victim, as the scapegoat was used among the Jews.

debates.question: The sense of 'Azazel' and the live goat

sides.position: The dismissed/sent-away goat, a common noun (Clarke)
A place/precipice in the wilderness, or an evil spirit dwelling there

themes: presented alive before the LORD
to let him go for a scapegoat into the wilderness
sin carried clean away, out of sight

schools.summary: Gill: the bullock of the sin offering which is for himself, to be understood in the same sense as v.6.

Ellicott: Aaron comes back to his own sin offering a second time, lays both hands on it, repeats the confession for himself, his house and the whole priesthood, and kills it; his own sins had first to be expiated before he could offer for the people (cf. Heb. 9:7).

themes: the bullock of the sin offering which is for himself
both hands laid on, sin confessed
the mediator cleansed before he cleanses

schools.summary: Gill: the golden censer used by the high priest on this day; Christ the Angel of God's presence, our interceding High Priest, is said to have such a censer (Rev. 8:3).

Keil & Delitzsch: vv.12-15 form the inmost service — the censer of coals and handful of beaten incense brought within the veil so the cloud might cover the mercy seat, then the blood of the bullock sprinkled.

themes: a censer full of burning coals... sweet incense beaten small
bring it within the vail
the incense figuring Christ's intercession

schools.summary: Gill: against the Sadducees (who held the incense was kindled without the veil), the incense was put on the coals within the veil before the LORD; the cloud was to cover the mercy seat that the high priest die not, and he was sworn to make no alteration in the rite.

debates.question: Was the incense kindled within or without the veil?

sides.position: Within the veil, before the LORD (the rite as given)

themes: the cloud of the incense may cover the mercy seat
that he die not
the merciful screen of Christ's intercession

schools.summary: Gill: the blood of the bullock was kept stirred lest it congeal, then carried in a second time after the incense and sprinkled with the finger upon and before the mercy seat.
Ellicott: in the second temple the high priest sprinkled once upward and seven times downward, the eight forming a continuous line, counting each lest he make a mistake.
Wesley: the blood upon the mercy seat teaches that God is merciful to sinners through the blood of atonement.

themes: sprinkle it... upon the mercy seat eastward... seven times
the blood at the throne of God
God merciful to sinners through atoning blood (Rom. 3:25)

schools.summary: Gill: the goat for the people was slain on the north of the altar where all sin offerings were killed — a type of Christ slain and made an offering for the sins of his people.

Henry: the priest came out from sprinkling the bullock's blood, killed the people's goat, and went a third time into the holy of holies to sprinkle its blood and make atonement for the holy place, that God being reconciled might continue with them.

themes: kill the goat of the sin offering that is for the people
bring his blood within the vail
Christ entering by his own blood into the holiest (Heb. 9:12)

schools.summary: Gill: the 'holy place' here is the holy of holies, atonement made by the blood of the bullock and goat sprinkled toward the mercy seat.

Keil & Delitzsch: the holy things were rendered unclean by the bodily manifestations of the nation's sin and so required a yearly expiation and cleansing through the blood of sacrifice, the tabernacle dwelling among them in the midst of their uncleanness.

themes: make an atonement for the holy place
because of the uncleanness of the children of Israel
the sanctuary itself needing cleansing (Heb. 9:23)

schools.summary: Gill: all places were cleared — not any of the priests in the holy place or court, nor any of the people; the high priest made atonement alone.

Keil & Delitzsch: no one was to be in the tabernacle when Aaron went in, not because none was worthy to witness, but because no unholy person was to defile by his presence the sanctuary just cleansed.

themes: there shall be no man in the tabernacle
the high priest atoning alone
Christ who trod the winepress alone (Isa. 63:3)

schools.summary: Gill: takes the altar as the golden altar of incense, on whose horns atonement was made once a year (Exod. 30:10), the high priest going out from the sanctuary; the blood of bullock and goat mingled and put on the horns round about.

sides.position: The golden altar of incense within
The brazen altar of burnt offering without

themes: go out unto the altar... make an atonement for it
blood of bullock and goat upon the horns
even the altar needing cleansing blood

schools.summary: Gill: the sprinkling was done with the right forefinger, seven times, to denote the perfect cleansing of the altar.
Keil & Delitzsch: he sprinkled the blood seven times to cleanse and to hallow the altar from the uncleanness of the children of Israel.

themes: sprinkle... with his finger seven times
cleanse it, and hallow it
the blood both cleansing and sanctifying (Heb. 10:14)

schools.summary: Gill: 'reconciling the holy place' is the carrying-in and sprinkling of the blood of the bullock and the goat in the holy of holies.
Henry: the expiatory blood being presented, the remainder poured out at the foot of the brazen altar, the high priest is next to confess Israel's sins over the scapegoat.

themes: made an end of reconciling... he shall bring the live goat
the blood-work finished first
sin borne away resting on atonement made

schools.summary: Gill: the laying of both hands (the right upon the left) on the live goat transferred the people's sins and their punishment to it, done in the name of the people.

Keil & Delitzsch: Aaron confessed all the iniquities, transgressions, and sins of Israel, putting them on the head of the goat, which was sent away by the hand of an appointed man into the wilderness.

themes: lay both his hands upon the head of the live goat
confess... all the iniquities
the LORD laying our iniquity on Christ (Isa. 53:6)

schools.summary: Gill: a proper type of Christ, who bore all the sins of his people in his own body on the cross, removed them as far as the east is from the west, out of the sight of avenging justice, never to return.

Ellicott: 'a land not inhabited' is literally 'a land cut off,' a place separated from all around; in the second temple the authorities decreed the goat be destroyed at a rock called Zuck, about twelve miles from Jerusalem.

themes: bear upon him all their iniquities unto a land not inhabited
let him go in the wilderness
sin carried clean away, never returning (Mic. 7:19)

schools.summary: Gill: relays the rabbinic view that Aaron came in to fetch the censer and incense cup, the section not being strictly in its order of time; he put off the linen and left it there.
Ellicott: the linen garments were worn only for the expiatory ritual of the day and then put off and left in the holy place.

themes: put off the linen garments
and shall leave them there
the humiliation belonging to the atonement alone

schools.summary: Gill: the washing was the dipping of the whole body in water; he ventures that if our Lord was baptized on this day there would be a likeness between type and antitype.
Keil & Delitzsch: he washed his flesh, put on his garments, came forth, and offered his burnt offering and the people's, making atonement for himself and for the people.

themes: wash his flesh... offer his burnt offering, and the burnt offering of the people
atonement issuing in devotion
pardon issuing in consecration

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: the fat on the inwards of both the bullock and the goat for the sin offering was burned upon the outward altar at the same time as the burnt offerings.
Ellicott: the fat of the inwards of both the bullock and the goat, which constituted the sin offering, was burned upon the altar.

themes: the fat of the sin offering shall he burn upon the altar
the choicest part to God
atonement ending in honour to God

schools.summary: Gill: distinguishes the goat from Azazel, holding they are not the same nor to be confounded.

Clarke: the man who led the goat away was reckoned unclean because the goat itself was unclean, being considered as bearing the sins of the whole congregation; therefore he had to wash his clothes and bathe before coming into the camp.

themes: he that let go the goat... shall wash his clothes
made unclean by the sin-bearing goat
the real weight of guilt on the substitute

schools.summary: Gill: the bullock for Aaron and his family and the goat for the people had their bodies carried without the camp and burned in the fire.
Keil & Delitzsch: the sin offerings whose blood was brought into the holy place to make atonement were carried forth without the camp and burned — skins, flesh, and dung.

themes: carry forth without the camp... burn in the fire
whose blood was brought in to make atonement
Jesus suffered without the gate (Heb. 13:11-12)

schools.summary: Gill: these being sin offerings, connected with the sins of men, those concerned in carrying and burning them were equally defiled and needed washing.
Ellicott: those who carried the carcases and burned them, like the messenger who led away the goat, contracted defilement from the atoning victims and had to undergo the same ablutions.

themes: he that burneth them shall wash his clothes
defiled by the atoning victims
the weight of sin resting on the victims

schools.summary: Gill: the statute was binding so long as the Aaronic priesthood and Levitical dispensation lasted, until the true Messiah came and put an end to these rites.
Henry: the day was kept yearly on the tenth of the seventh month, the people afflicting their souls by fasting and humiliation, with rest from all work.

themes: a statute for ever... the tenth day of the month
ye shall afflict your souls
the day of pardon a day of humbling

schools.summary: Gill: typical of the sacrifice of Christ, whose soul was made an offering for sin and whose blood cleanses from all sin; the priest a type of Christ our High Priest who made reconciliation.
Keil & Delitzsch: on that day the anointed priest made atonement to cleanse the people from all their sins before the LORD, once a year.

themes: make an atonement for you, to cleanse you
clean from all your sins before the LORD
the blood of Christ purging the conscience (Heb. 9:14)

schools.summary: Gill: the rest is typical of ceasing from sinful works, and from dependence on works of righteousness, when a man is brought to believe in Christ and the atonement he has made.
Ellicott: 'a sabbath of rest' is literally 'a sabbath of sabbaths,' a day of complete and perfect rest, a phrase applied only to weekly sabbaths, the Day of Atonement, and the year of jubilee.

themes: a sabbath of rest... ye shall afflict your souls
a sabbath of sabbaths
the soul resting in the atonement, kept humble

schools.summary: Gill: he whom God should anoint, succeeding in the high priesthood as Aaron's eldest sons did, was to make atonement as Aaron had done.
Ellicott: 'the priest who shall be anointed,' the duly consecrated successor to the pontificate, was in future to perform this act of expiation on the Day of Atonement.

themes: the priest, whom he shall anoint... shall make the atonement
in his father's stead
a succession of dying priests (Heb. 7:23)

schools.summary: Gill: the 'holy sanctuary' is the holy of holies, atoned for in the same manner as Aaron did.

Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott: the verse is the summary of the whole chapter — atonement for the sanctuary, the tabernacle, the altar, the priests, and all the people — the successor performing all as detailed.

themes: an atonement for the holy sanctuary... the priests, and... all the people
every part embraced
the comprehensive reach of Christ's atonement

schools.summary: Gill: the third time the statute is called everlasting, a law of weight to be strictly kept; Aaron did according to this order when the day of atonement came, to fulfil the decree of the King of kings.
Ellicott: renders 'a statute for ever,' noting the same phrase occurs three times within a few verses.

themes: an everlasting statute... once a year
atonement repeated yearly
Christ's one offering once for all (Heb. 9:25-26)
